era_context: 1828 was a year of military conflict, political consolidation, and technological innovation. The Russo-Turkish War (1828–1829) was being fought in the Eastern Mediterranean and Caucasus. In the United States, Andrew Jackson was elected president, signalling a shift in American politics. In Europe, the conservative order established by the Congress of Vienna (1815) remained dominant, but liberal and nationalist movements were gathering strength. In Asia, the Qing Dynasty continued to dominate, but European colonial expansion was accelerating. The year marked the beginning of significant changes that would reshape the political landscape of the 1830s.

events.title: Russo-Turkish War Begins
events.description: The Russo-Turkish War began in 1828, fought over Russian expansion in the Caucasus and the Balkans, and Ottoman support for Greek independence. The war was part of a broader pattern of Russian expansion at Ottoman expense. Russian forces, under the command of General Ivan Diebitsch, invaded Ottoman territories in the Balkans and Caucasus. The war lasted until 1829, when the Treaty of Adrianople was signed, resulting in significant Russian territorial gains and Ottoman territorial losses. The war demonstrated the declining military power of the Ottoman Empire relative to Russia and foreshadowed the 'Eastern Question' that would dominate European diplomacy in the 19th century.

sources.name: Russian and Ottoman military records
Barbara Jelavich, History of the Balkans; Donald Quataert, The Ottoman Empire 1700–1922

events.certainty_note: The beginning of the Russo-Turkish War in 1828 is well-documented in Russian and Ottoman military records.
cross_references: Leads to 1829-003 (Treaty of Adrianople ends the war)

events.title: Andrew Jackson Elected U.S. President
events.description: Andrew Jackson was elected the seventh President of the United States in November 1828, defeating the incumbent John Quincy Adams. Jackson's election represented a significant shift in American politics, marking the rise of the Democratic Party and the decline of the old Federalist-Republican establishment. Jackson's campaign appealed to ordinary Americans and emphasised populist themes. His election was celebrated by his supporters as a victory for democracy and condemned by his opponents as a threat to established institutions. Jackson would be inaugurated on 4 March 1829 and would serve two terms (1829–1837).
key_figures: Martin Van Buren (Jackson supporter, later Vice President)

sources.name: U.S. election records and contemporary newspapers
Sean Wilentz, The Rise of American Democracy; Daniel Walker Howe, What Hath God Wrought

events.certainty_note: Jackson's election in November 1828 is well-documented in election records and contemporary sources.

events.title: Catholic Emancipation Debate Intensifies in Britain
events.description: In 1828, the debate over Catholic Emancipation intensified in Britain. The election of Daniel O'Connell, an Irish Catholic, to Parliament in 1828 (though he could not take his seat due to religious restrictions) highlighted the injustice of the existing system. The Duke of Wellington's government faced pressure to grant civil and political rights to Catholics. The debate would culminate in the passage of the Catholic Emancipation Act in 1829. The 1828 election of O'Connell was a turning point in the campaign for Catholic rights.

sources.name: Parliamentary debates and records (Hansard, 1828)
Contemporary newspapers and political commentary
Peter Jupp, The Governing of Britain 1688–1848

events.certainty_note: The intensification of the Catholic Emancipation debate in 1828 is well-documented in parliamentary records and contemporary sources.

events.title: Slavery Debate Intensifies in United States
events.description: In 1828, the debate over slavery intensified in the United States, particularly following the 'Tariff of Abominations' (a high protective tariff passed by Congress). Southern states, which relied on slavery and agricultural exports, opposed the tariff, which they believed harmed their economic interests. The tariff debate became entangled with the slavery question, as Northern industrial interests and Southern agricultural interests clashed. The debate would continue through the 1830s and lead to the Nullification Crisis (1832–1833) and eventually to the Civil War (1861–1865). The 1828 election of Andrew Jackson, a slave owner, reflected the political power of the South.
key_figures: John C. Calhoun (South Carolina politician, slavery defender)

sources.name: Congressional debates and records (1828)
Contemporary newspapers and political commentary
Sean Wilentz, The Rise of American Democracy

events.certainty_note: The intensification of the slavery debate in 1828 is well-documented in congressional records and contemporary sources.

events.title: First Passenger Railway Opens in Britain (Stockton-Darlington)
events.description: The Stockton and Darlington Railway, which opened on 27 September 1825, was the first public railway to use steam locomotives. However, 1828 saw the continued development and expansion of railway technology in Britain. The Liverpool-Manchester Railway was under construction and would open in 1830, becoming the first successful passenger railway line. The development of railway technology in the late 1820s was rapid, with George Stephenson and others making significant innovations. The railway revolution would transform transportation, commerce, and society in the 19th century.

sources.name: Contemporary engineering records and newspaper accounts
Christian Wolmar, The Great Railway Bazaar; Jack Simmons, The Victorian Railway

events.certainty_note: The development of railway technology in 1828 is well-documented in engineering records and contemporary sources.

events.title: Cholera Pandemic Spreads from Bengal
events.description: The cholera pandemic that began in Bengal in 1817 continued to spread in 1828, moving westward through Central Asia and toward Russia. The disease had killed millions across Asia and was beginning to reach Europe. The pandemic would reach Russia in 1830 and Europe in 1831–1832. The spread of cholera in 1828 was facilitated by trade routes and military movements. The pandemic was one of the most significant public health crises of the 19th century.

sources.name: Medical records and government reports from affected regions
Charles E. Rosenberg, The Cholera Years

events.certainty_note: The spread of cholera in 1828 is documented in medical records and government reports from affected regions.

events.title: Qing Dynasty Faces Internal Unrest and Economic Challenges
events.description: In 1828, the Qing Dynasty under the Daoguang Emperor faced mounting economic and military challenges. Population growth, land scarcity, and economic inequality created social tensions. The Qing military, once formidable, was beginning to show signs of decline relative to European powers. Piracy and banditry were increasing in coastal and inland regions. These challenges foreshadowed the larger crises (Opium Wars, Taiping Rebellion) that would destabilise the dynasty in the following decades. The Daoguang Emperor's reign (1820–1850) was marked by increasing fiscal strain and military challenges.

sources.name: Qing imperial records and provincial reports
Jonathan D. Spence, The Search for Modern China

events.certainty_note: The broader context of Qing challenges in 1828 is well-established, but specific events are less detailed than major crises.

events.title: Greek War of Independence Continues
events.description: The Greek War of Independence (1821–1829) continued in 1828, with Greek forces fighting against Ottoman rule. European powers (Britain, France, Russia) provided support to the Greek cause, partly for strategic reasons and partly due to Philhellenic sentiment among European intellectuals and politicians. The war would end in 1829 with the Treaty of Adrianople, which recognised Greek independence. The Greek War of Independence was one of the first successful nationalist uprisings against European imperial rule and inspired other nationalist movements in the 19th century.

sources.name: Greek and Ottoman military records
David Brewer, The Greek War of Independence; Barbara Jelavich, History of the Balkans

events.certainty_note: The Greek War of Independence in 1828 is well-documented in military records and contemporary sources.
cross_references: Part of broader Greek War of Independence (1821–1829)
Leads to 1829-003 (Treaty of Adrianople ends the war)

events.title: Slavery Abolished in Chile
events.description: In 1828, Chile abolished slavery as part of broader liberal reforms following independence from Spain (1810). The abolition was part of a wave of anti-slavery legislation in Latin America in the 1820s–1830s. However, slavery persisted in some regions and was not fully enforced. The abolition of slavery in Chile and other Latin American nations contrasted with the persistence of slavery in the United States and Brazil, which would not abolish slavery until 1865 and 1888, respectively.

sources.name: Chilean government records and decrees
Jaime E. Rodríguez O., The Independence of Spanish America

events.certainty_note: The abolition of slavery in Chile in 1828 is documented in Chilean government records.
cross_references: Part of broader Latin American abolition of slavery (1810s–1830s)

events.title: Tariff of Abominations Passed by U.S. Congress
events.description: The Tariff of 1828, known as the 'Tariff of Abominations' by its Southern opponents, was passed by the U.S. Congress. The tariff imposed high duties on imported goods, particularly raw materials and manufactured goods. The tariff was designed to protect Northern industrial interests but was opposed by Southern agricultural interests, which relied on free trade to export cotton and other agricultural products. The tariff became a symbol of Northern economic dominance and Southern economic subordination. The tariff debate became entangled with the slavery question and contributed to the Nullification Crisis (1832–1833) and eventually to the Civil War (1861–1865).
key_figures: John C. Calhoun (South Carolina politician, opponent of the tariff)

sources.name: U.S. Congressional Records (1828)
The Tariff of 1828 (text)
Sean Wilentz, The Rise of American Democracy

events.certainty_note: The passage of the Tariff of 1828 is well-documented in congressional records.

disconfirming_evidence: The Russo-Turkish War is sometimes portrayed as a Russian triumph, but it also reflected Ottoman military strength and the limits of Russian expansion. The election of Andrew Jackson is sometimes celebrated as a triumph of democracy, but Jackson was a slave owner and his presidency led to the Indian Removal Act, which resulted in the deaths of thousands of Native Americans. The development of railway technology is sometimes presented as purely beneficial, but it also facilitated the expansion of capitalism and colonialism. The abolition of slavery in Latin America is sometimes celebrated, but slavery persisted in practice in many regions and was not fully enforced.
historiographic_note: 1828 is well-documented in European and North American sources, reflecting the dominance of these regions in surviving archives. The year is rich in political, military, and economic events. However, documentation for Asia, Africa, and the Americas outside the United States is thinner. The Qing Dynasty's challenges are attested in imperial records but are less detailed than major rebellions. African and indigenous American perspectives are underrepresented in surviving sources. The sources for 1828 are predominantly written by educated elites (government officials, military officers, intellectuals), so popular experiences and non-literate societies are largely absent from the record.

graph_edges.note: The Russo-Turkish War led to the Treaty of Adrianople in 1829
Jackson's election led to his inauguration as president in 1829
The Catholic Emancipation debate of 1828 led to the passage of the act in 1829
The slavery debate of 1828 contributed to the Nullification Crisis of 1832
The spread of cholera in 1828 led to its arrival in Russia in 1830
